summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is missing a crucial component: a centralized metrics layer that defines and serves consistent metric logic across all tools. Without it, self-serve analytics devolves into fragmented, inconsistent calculations, and BI tools end up as isolated silos rather than shared infrastructure. A proper metrics layer would make metrics universally accessible via SQL, unify reporting with deeper analysis, and complete the stack’s foundation.
money_quotes: Except for one: a metrics layer.
Today’s current stack makes it easy to answer this question, but really hard to answer it consistently.
The core problem is that there’s no central repository for defining a metric.
A better architecture would do for metrics what dbt did for transformed data—make them globally accessible to every other tool in the data stack.

That, ultimately, is the biggest benefit of a metrics layer: It completes the foundation on which operational BI and exploratory data science both live, bringing these two functions together in ways they can’t be combined today.
key_insight: A centralized, SQL-addressable metrics layer is the missing foundation needed to make self-serve analytics consistent across the modern data stack.
